item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suppose the truth is: he never needed to escape. His consciousness was already spread across so many bodies; the problem was that the Foundation only had GRU's notes to work with and turns out they were just as much in the dark. He kept a body in the care of GRU and then the Foundation, as a way to observe them. A reason he appears so nonchalant about his captivity. For him, it was like losing a finger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God that ending still is freaky, they really just yeet that guy into flesh. So good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there any significance to his birth being the same year as the Fourth Crusade?

Serf, szlachta, and boyar

Maybe include footnotes for szlachta and boyar? Most people probably wouldn't know what they are.

That is one very long escape plan.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the use of foreshadowing in this article, where the skip tells the doc about it hearing about his wife. Very well written with many groups tied into it. +1 for me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really love the perpetual struggle between the Sarkic and the CotBG, and this is one of the best Sarkic articles I've had the pleasure of reading. Very well-written, especially the dialogue. Enthusiastic +1 from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what a crazy ass ending, sarkic shit is so cool. 1+

item.title: Mod Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Toma_nikolov_hristov.jpg

Image Source, Public Domain

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of the better works on the site in terms of characterization and worldbuilding, which is a combination I find rare here. The character of the dude comes through clear as day, and the construction of the Sarkic universe here is just sublime. The narrative bulk of the article hooks me in and doesn't let go.

Happily +1'd

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I saw chlorine trifluoride and instantly squeed.
in addition to being intrigued by how much this reminds me of the Thorian from Mass Effect.
Love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry for being a bit of an English cop.

In the next to last paragraph, it describes the Ouroboros as being "believed" to be a symbol of religious significance. Does that mean they simply haven't researched enough to know it is, or should it just say that it "is" a symbol of religious significance?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: {nevermind}

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quite enjoyable, although none of this was hugely surprising. Also, doing a body horror article with a doctor named "Cronenberg" is like doing an article with a man-spider and a doctor named "Ditko." Still, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Belated response: This guy is a Karcist, not Ion - here is merely quoting the words of Ion. (Fun fact: The image of Ion in 2480? In my head, he isn't the real Ion either - "Pseudo-Ion" is what I call him; either a powerful Neo-Sarkic karcist masquerading as the real deal or a manifestation of sorts, a "shade of Ion" that isn't one and the same as the Ion bound to Adytum in the "other side". Though, his outfit and everything is probably accurate so the real Ion would look somewhat like him)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really good! But is this guy actually Ion, or is ion just speaking through him? Also, it would be fun to get an Anomalous Item profile of the ceremonial dagger.

And yes, I still think that the Serpent's hand reference is gratutious.

Overall, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just realized that 2075 mentioned the Serpent's Hand. Does that mean that the SH will take a part in the grander Sarkicism mythos?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted, but, being Russian, would like to propose a minor change to the surname. Kravchuv does not really sound as a natural russian last name, rather Polish or Serbian. Closest variants might be Kravchuk, Kravcev or Krivtsov.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this, but the twist was kind of obvious from the get-go. Maybe it's just that I've seen The Thing, but I don't know. Speaking of which, this was basically just The Thing as a Russian priest. I'm not sure if that was your inspiration, or if you've even seen that movie, but that was what I got from it. I thought some of the GoI name drops were totally pointless. I'd personally takeout the Serpent's Hand one out entirely.

Overall, it was good enough to merit an upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
sekrit hidden scp document

Nooooop stoooop I don't caaaaaaaare

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thing is, the blob of flesh he's turning into is most likely another one of these. I could see why the Foundation might be willing to completely destroy a section of their site with highly destructive chemical flooding to keep that fiasco from happening again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sector-G was flooded with chlorine trifluoride

Loved the SCP, but nobody is ever going to choose to flood anything with ClF3.

It is, of course, extremely toxic, but that's the least of the problem. It is hypergolic with every known fuel, and so rapidly hypergolic that no ignition delay has ever been measured. It is also hypergolic with such things as cloth, wood, and test engineers, not to mention asbestos, sand, and water — with which it reacts explosively. It can be kept in some of the ordinary structural metals — steel, copper, aluminum, etc. — because of the formation of a thin film of insoluble metal fluoride which protects the bulk of the metal, just as the invisible coat of oxide on aluminum keeps it from burning up in the atmosphere. If, however, this coat is melted or scrubbed off, and has no chance to reform, the operator is confronted with the problem of coping with a metal-fluorine fire. For dealing with this situation, I have always recommended a good pair of running shoes.

Even keeping this stuff at the same site as anomalies are contained is asking for containment breach, this stuff is industrial accident central and burning through all the concrete surrounding Sector G while causing explosions and massive production of Hydrofluoric and hydrochloric acide may sound like a good reaction to one researcher turning into a blob of flesh, it is a little violent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't this tagged uncontained?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://fc00.deviantart.net/fs70/i/2014/350/e/a/scp_2075_by_sunnyparallax-d8a1rxr.jpg

Thanks to Sunnyparallax, there is now a piece of art for this!:)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ha, thanks for pointing that out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Heavy causalities were suffered"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for pointing it out. Fixed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If we think he's possibly 700-something, where do we get the "sixty-three years old" figure from?

Edit: Wait, no, I get it after reading the logs.

I think you made a formally/formerly error, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These Flesh vs. CotBG scips just get more and more interesting.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation had him for 13 years, and the security personnel had over 10 years tenure with the Foundation. That's a long term escape plan! I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks:) Wanted to deal with the difficulty of containing a multiform being, especially when those who originally contained it have long since died. Like caging the tentacle of an octopus and assuming the whole octopus has been secured;p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Allright, this makes it substantially better. But, eh, upvoted already so =^.^=

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It never took over the Foundation security guards. All the security measures worked.

They were part of him when they joined; a multi-form entity with a *lot* of patience.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm. I think I like this despite the GRU-P inclusion being at large without substance, CotBG being namesdropped, and despite some misgivings about what happened - what I thought to be the reveal (that all we have contained is one puppet of many) is perhaps better than what really happened (that it rather primitively took over a bunch of Foundation security guards after its properties seem to have been known enough to prevent it.)
Somewhat reluctant +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
GRU-P with a mention of CotBG? Interesting.

I have to say I was a little underwhelmed by the reveal at the end. It was pretty obvious, if this guy were to be taken at face value, that that was indeed the case. The only really interesting part is when he reveals his identity. Still a decent humanoid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Corrected that! Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uralic! Thanks! I corrected it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a much better article to tie into your SCP-2095 mythos. However, the mythos is only enough to disguise a killer, mutating hive mind. I actually preferred the cover documentation to the actual documentation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2075 is able to exert its anomalous affects regardless of distance after initial infection

"Affect" is possible here, and in one interpretation it's plausible in context, but it's a rather obscure usage. Are you sure you don't mean "Effect"?

I like the historical and theological details - and the use of the name Cronenberg. Also the indications that the SCP articles we see are condensed from an implied much larger body of documents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You say Ularic and Uralic in the same sentence, this seems to be a typo.

Although, I, myself, am unsure of which is correct.
